summary:
The Viking voyage to Vinland (c. 1000–1020 AD) — the Norse discovery and brief settlement of North America, roughly 500 years before Columbus — represents the first confirmed European contact with the Americas. Leif Eriksson, son of [[event:2146|Erik the Red]] of Greenland, sailed west from Greenland (reportedly after being blown off course, or following the account of a trader named Bjarni who had sighted land) and landed in 'Vinland' (Wine Land, named for the grapes or berries found there), 'Markland' (Forest Land), and 'Helluland' (Flat Stone Land) — locations most likely corresponding to Newfoundland, Labrador, and Baffin Island.

The Viking presence in North America was confirmed conclusively in 1960 when Norwegian explorer Helge Ingstad and archaeologist Anne Stine Ingstad discovered a Norse site at L'Anse aux Meadows in northern Newfoundland — the only confirmed Norse site in North America outside Greenland, featuring turf longhouses of distinctly Norse construction. The Vinland Sagas (Greenlanders' Saga and Eirik's Saga) describe multiple voyages, attempted settlement, and conflict with indigenous peoples ('Skraelings') that ultimately forced abandonment. The Norse did not establish permanent settlement in North America, probably because they lacked the numbers and technology to overcome indigenous resistance, and the distance from their Greenland base limited supply. Nevertheless, the Vinland voyages demonstrate that trans-Atlantic contact was technologically possible centuries before Columbus.
